sourceTitle: How to build a wiki for your design team

questions.question: What is the main reason a design team should use a wiki?

options.text: To store all design assets in one central, up‑to‑date location
options.explanation: A wiki serves as a single source of truth for assets, guidelines, and processes, ensuring everyone accesses the latest versions.

options.text: To replace all design tools like Figma and Sketch
options.explanation: Notion complements design tools; it doesn’t replace specialized design software.

options.text: To limit collaboration only to senior designers
options.explanation: A wiki is meant for the whole team (and sometimes the whole company), not just senior members.

options.text: To automatically generate brand assets without manual input
options.explanation: Notion stores assets but doesn’t generate them automatically.

questions.question: Which action creates a new page in Notion when you are already inside another page?

options.text: Press the '+' button next to Workspace in the sidebar
options.explanation: That creates a top‑level page, not a sub‑page from within another page.

options.text: Type '/' and select 'page', then press Enter
options.explanation: Typing '/' opens the slash command menu; selecting 'page' creates a new sub‑page at the cursor location.

options.text: Click the 'Share' button at the top right
options.explanation: The Share button is for permissions, not page creation.

options.text: Drag an existing page onto the canvas
options.explanation: Dragging moves pages; it doesn’t create a new one.

questions.question: Which slash command lets you add a visual gallery database to a page?

options.text: /gallery
options.explanation: Typing '/gallery' creates a Gallery database, which displays cards visually.

options.text: /table
options.explanation: '/table' creates a Table database, not a visual gallery.

options.text: /board
options.explanation: There is no '/board' command; Kanban boards are created via '/board view' inside a database.

options.text: /embed
options.explanation: '/embed' lets you embed external content, not create a gallery.

questions.question: Why is having a single source of truth crucial for a design team’s brand consistency?

options.text: It guarantees that every team member uses the same up‑to‑date assets and guidelines, preventing brand drift.
options.explanation: When all assets live in one place, designers always pull the latest versions, which maintains visual consistency across the brand.

options.text: It allows designers to work offline without ever syncing files.
options.explanation: A central wiki still requires internet access to retrieve the latest files; offline work isn’t its primary benefit.

options.text: It eliminates the need for any design review process.
options.explanation: Reviews are still necessary; the wiki just provides the reference material for those reviews.

options.text: It automatically updates the brand’s website whenever a file changes.
options.explanation: Notion doesn’t push changes to external websites automatically.

questions.question: What advantage does nesting sub‑pages inside a design wiki provide?

options.text: It keeps related resources together, making it faster for designers to locate assets and documentation.
options.explanation: Nested pages create a logical hierarchy, reducing search time and keeping context together.

options.text: It automatically generates a PDF version of each sub‑page.
options.explanation: Notion doesn’t auto‑export PDFs for sub‑pages; you must export manually.

options.text: It locks the sub‑pages so only admins can edit them.
options.explanation: Nesting does not affect permissions; edit rights are set separately.

options.text: It duplicates the content of the parent page into each sub‑page.
options.explanation: Content isn’t duplicated; each sub‑page is independent unless you add blocks manually.

questions.question: How can headers be used to organize content within a Notion page?

options.text: Create H1, H2, or H3 blocks and then drag pages or blocks under them to form sections.
options.explanation: Headers act as collapsible sections; you can nest pages or blocks under them for clear organization.

options.text: Headers automatically generate a table of contents without any further action.
options.explanation: A table of contents block must be added manually; headers alone don’t create it.

options.text: Headers change the page’s permission settings based on their level.
options.explanation: Headers have no effect on permissions.

options.text: Headers convert the page into a database view.
options.explanation: Headers are formatting tools, not database creators.

questions.question: Which of the following content types can be embedded directly into a Notion page for design teams?

options.text: Figma files, PDFs, and Loom videos
options.explanation: Notion supports embedding Figma prototypes, PDFs, Loom videos, and many other design‑related formats.

options.text: Adobe Photoshop PSD files as editable layers
options.explanation: Notion can display a preview of a PSD, but it cannot edit layers directly.

options.text: Microsoft Excel macros
options.explanation: Excel macros aren’t supported for embedding; you can embed a static spreadsheet view instead.

options.text: AutoCAD 3D models with live manipulation
options.explanation: Notion doesn’t provide live 3D manipulation for AutoCAD files.

questions.question: How can you share a Notion page with a client so they can view it but not edit or comment?

options.text: Turn on 'Share to the web' and leave editing and commenting disabled
options.explanation: Enabling 'Share to the web' creates a public link; if you don’t allow editing or commenting, the client can only view.

options.text: Invite the client as a guest with 'Can edit' permissions
options.explanation: Giving 'Can edit' allows the client to modify the page, which is not what the question asks.

options.text: Add the client’s email to the page’s 'Members' list and set the role to 'Viewer'
options.explanation: Notion does not have a distinct 'Viewer' role; you must use the web share option for view‑only access.

options.text: Export the page as a PDF and email it to the client
options.explanation: Exporting as PDF provides a static snapshot, not a live Notion view.

questions.question: Why does using a Gallery database make a design wiki more visual than a regular page list?

options.text: Each entry appears as a card that can show a cover image or page content, letting designers browse assets visually.
options.explanation: Gallery view displays items as image‑rich cards, which is ideal for visual assets like logos, typography, or mood boards.

options.text: Gallery databases automatically generate color palettes based on uploaded images.
options.explanation: Notion does not auto‑generate color palettes; it only displays the images you provide.

options.text: The Gallery view disables all text content, focusing solely on images.
options.explanation: You can still show text properties; the view is flexible, not text‑only disabled.

options.text: Cards in a Gallery cannot be rearranged, ensuring a fixed visual order.
options.explanation: Cards can be dragged to reorder; the view is fully editable.

questions.question: When giving a client the ability to edit a Notion page, which sequence of steps is required?

options.text: Invite the client as a guest via the Share menu, grant them editing rights, and ensure they have a Notion account.
options.explanation: On Team or Pro plans you can add a client as a guest, set their permission to 'Can edit', and they must be logged into Notion to edit.

options.text: Turn on 'Share to the web' and toggle the 'Allow editing' switch; no invitation is needed.
options.explanation: While this makes the page publicly editable, it’s not the recommended secure method for client collaboration.

options.text: Copy the page, paste it into the client’s personal workspace, and give them admin rights.
options.explanation: Copying to another workspace isn’t necessary and admin rights are excessive for simple editing.

options.text: Export the page as a Word document, send it to the client, and ask them to re‑import it.
options.explanation: Exporting loses the live Notion functionality and does not allow direct editing within Notion.
